sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255
sections.title: Digital advice definition and scope
sections.summary: This section defines digital advice as automated financial product advice using algorithms and technology without direct human involvement. It clarifies that digital advice can range from narrow scope advice (e.g., portfolio construction) to comprehensive financial planning. The definition distinguishes digital advice from traditional personal advice and specifies that it applies to retail clients.

requirements.text: Digital advice (also known as 'robo-advice' or 'automated advice') is the provision of automated financial product advice using algorithms and technology and without the direct involvement of a human adviser.
Digital advice can comprise general or personal advice, and range from advice that is narrow in scope (e.g. advice about portfolio construction) to a comprehensive financial plan.

sections.source_quote: Digital advice (also known as robo-advice or automated advice) is the provision of automated financial product advice using algorithms and technology and without the direct involvement of a human adviser.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: defines digital advice scope but not core data compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: ASIC regulatory document types and history
sections.summary: This section explains the types of regulatory documents ASIC issues, including consultation papers, regulatory guides, information sheets, and reports. It details the document's history, noting minor updates in 2021 and 2022 to reflect changes in fee arrangements, training requirements, and breach reporting reforms. The document emphasizes that it does not constitute legal advice and is illustrative only.

requirements.text: ASIC issues regulatory documents such as consultation papers, regulatory guides, information sheets, and reports.
Regulatory guides explain when and how ASIC will exercise specific powers under legislation, interpret the law, describe principles, and provide practical guidance.
This guide was issued in August 2016 and is based on legislation and regulations as at the date of issue.
Minor updates were made in June 2021 to Table 1 in the Appendix to reflect changes to ongoing fee arrangements.
RG 255.51 was updated in June 2021 to reflect changes to training, education, and ethical standards for financial advisers.
One sample question at RG 255.41 and RG 255.115 was updated in March 2022 to reflect breach reporting reforms effective from 1 October 2021.
This guide does not constitute legal advice; users must seek their own professional advice to determine obligations.

sections.source_quote: Regulatory guides: give guidance to regulated entities by: explaining when and how ASIC will exercise specific powers under legislation (primarily the Corporations Act)...
sections.relevance_reason: pure amendment mechanics

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Regulatory guide structure and contents
sections.summary: This section outlines the structure of the regulatory guide, including sections on digital advice in Australia, AFS licensing requirements, general obligations for digital advice licensees, and providing scaled advice in the best interests of clients. It lists the guide's contents and related information, such as key terms and non-ASIC publications.

requirements.text: The guide includes an overview section on digital advice in Australia.
The guide covers scope of this regulatory guide.
The guide lists other relevant guidance.
The guide addresses digital advice and the AFS licensing regime.
The guide includes general obligations applying to digital advice licensees.
The guide covers providing scaled advice in the best interests of the client.
The guide provides an appendix with other ASIC guidance.

sections.relevance_reason: tangential: general governance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital advice definition and market context
sections.summary: This section defines digital advice as automated financial product advice without direct human involvement, clarifies its scope (from narrow to comprehensive advice), and notes its rapid growth in Australia since 2014. It emphasizes ASIC's support for a healthy digital advice market and the potential for digital advice to serve retail clients who do not seek traditional advice.

requirements.text: Digital advice (also known as 'robo-advice' or 'automated advice') is the provision of automated financial product advice using algorithms and technology and without the direct involvement of a human adviser.
Digital advice can comprise general or personal advice, and range from advice that is narrow in scope (e.g. advice about portfolio construction) to a comprehensive financial plan.

The provision of digital advice has grown rapidly in Australia since 2014, with a number of start-up Australian financial services (AFS) licensees and existing AFS licensees developing digital advice models.
ASIC supports the development of a healthy and robust digital advice market in Australia.

In an environment where only around 20% of adult Australians seek personal advice, digital advice has the potential to be a convenient and low-cost option for retail clients who may not otherwise seek advice.

sections.source_quote: Digital advice (also known as 'robo-advice' or 'automated advice') is the provision of automated financial product advice using algorithms and technology and without the direct involvement of a human adviser.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: market context for digital advice

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Scope and purpose of regulatory guide
sections.summary: This section explains that the regulatory guide builds on existing ASIC guidance without introducing new regulatory concepts, as the law is technology neutral. It clarifies the guide's scope, including its focus on obligations for digital advice providers and the need for digital advice licensees to comply with the best interests duty. It also addresses unique aspects of digital advice, such as organizational competence obligations and algorithm monitoring.

requirements.text: This regulatory guide generally builds on existing ASIC guidance and does not introduce new regulatory concepts.

This guide brings together some of the issues that you will need to consider if you want to provide digital advice when operating in Australia—from the licensing stage (i.e. obtaining an AFS licence) through to the actual provision of advice.
Our guidance focuses on the obligations of those providing, or wanting to provide, digital advice.
Businesses that provide associated services (e.g. technology or compliance services) may also find this guidance useful.
Those who provide combined (or hybrid) financial product advice models should also consider this guidance.
This guide provides guidance on how the organisational competence obligation in s912A(1)(e) of the Corporations Act applies to digital advice licensees.
The guide outlines ways in which digital advice licensees should monitor and test their algorithms.
The guide specifies minimum steps for digital advice providers to comply with the best interests duty when providing scaled advice.

sections.source_quote: This regulatory guide generally builds on existing ASIC guidance and does not introduce new regulatory concepts.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for digital advice compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Regulatory framework principles and compliance expectations
sections.summary: This section describes Australia's principles-based financial services regulatory regime, emphasizing that it does not prescribe specific compliance steps. It explains that ASIC provides guidance to help industry understand regulatory obligations, using terms like 'must' for legal obligations and 'expect' for recommended practices. It encourages digital advice providers to contact ASIC if they have alternative compliance methods.

requirements.text: Australia's financial services regulatory regime is principles based and does not prescribe the steps that an AFS licensee needs to take to comply with the law.
From engagement with industry, ASIC has issued this guide to assist in understanding regulatory obligations for digital advice providers.
In this guide, 'must' or 'required to' refers to legal obligations that must be complied with.
In this guide, 'expect' or 'should' refers to recommended practices for compliance.
AFS licensees will generally be able to comply with the law by adhering to the ways ASIC thinks they can comply.
Failure to do what is expected may result in further regulatory inquiry from ASIC.
ASIC is open to hearing about new or alternative ways to comply with the law.

sections.source_quote: Generally, Australia's financial services regulatory regime is principles based and does not prescribe the steps that an AFS licensee needs to take to comply with the law.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory guidance on compliance expectations

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital advice communication design and disclosure requirements
sections.summary: Digital advice providers must design communications and disclosures with a user-focused approach prioritizing client needs. They should consider device-specific contexts and implement ongoing testing to enhance communication effectiveness. This applies to all digital advice providers operating in Australia.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital advice providers must ensure communications are designed with client needs first and undergo ongoing testing to avoid misinterpretation of advice type. This affects how data is shared and disclosed across digital channels.

requirements.text: Digital advice providers must design communications and disclosures with a user-focused approach prioritizing client needs
Digital advice providers should consider device-specific contexts for communication effectiveness
Digital advice providers should implement ongoing testing to enhance communication effectiveness

sections.source_quote: Communication in a digital context: RG 255.15
sections.relevance_reason: tangential: user-focused design not core data protection

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: AFS licensing requirements for digital advice providers
sections.summary: Digital advice providers must hold an AFS licence or be an authorised representative of an AFS licensee unless exempted. This applies to all providers of financial product advice to retail clients. Providers must comply with conduct and disclosure obligations under the Corporations Act. Factual information provision does not require AFS licensing.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital advice providers must obtain AFS licensing before providing financial product advice, which affects data transfer and processing across borders due to licensing requirements. This section clarifies when AFS licensing is required for digital advice.

requirements.text: Digital advice providers must hold an AFS licence or act as an authorised representative of an AFS licensee unless exempted
Digital advice providers must comply with conduct and disclosure obligations under Pt 7.7 and Div 2 of Pt 7.7A
Factual information provision does not require AFS licensing

sections.source_quote: Under the Corporations Act, you are required to hold an AFS licence, or to be an authorised representative of an AFS licensee, if you are carrying on a financial services business, unless an exemption applies.
sections.relevance_reason: AFS licensing for financial advice affects data handling

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Definition of financial product advice types for digital services
sections.summary: Financial product advice is classified as either personal or general advice under the Corporations Act. Personal advice requires consideration of client objectives, financial situation, and needs. General advice does not consider these factors. Digital advice providers must determine whether their communications constitute personal or general advice to ensure appropriate licensing.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital advice providers must classify their communications as factual information, general advice, or personal advice to determine licensing requirements. This classification affects data processing and transfer obligations.

requirements.text: Financial product advice is either personal or general advice under the Corporations Act
Personal advice requires consideration of client objectives, financial situation, and needs
General advice does not consider client objectives, financial situation, or needs

sections.source_quote: Under the Corporations Act, all financial product advice is either 'personal advice' or 'general advice'.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for financial advice licensing

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Examples of digital advice classification and licensing requirements
sections.summary: Digital advice providers must determine whether their services constitute factual information, general advice, or personal advice. Examples show that providing recommendations or statements of opinion that influence client decisions requires AFS licensing. Providers must ensure their services meet the criteria for personal advice (considering client circumstances) or general advice (not considering client circumstances).
sections.dtia_summary: Digital advice providers must classify their services as factual information, general advice, or personal advice to determine AFS licensing requirements. This classification directly impacts data transfer and processing obligations.

requirements.text: Digital advice providers must determine whether their services constitute factual information, general advice, or personal advice
Services providing recommendations or statements of opinion that influence client decisions require AFS licensing
Personal advice requires consideration of client objectives, financial situation, and needs

sections.source_quote: If a communication is a recommendation or a statement of opinion, or a report of either of these things, that is intended to, or can reasonably be regarded as being intended to, influence a client in making a decision about a particular financial product or class of financial product (or an interest in either of these), it is financial product advice: s766B (see RG 244.27).
sections.relevance_reason: AFS licensing and advice classification impact data handling

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Exemptions and AFS licensing arrangements for digital advice
sections.summary: Digital advice providers may be exempt from AFS licensing if they provide generic financial calculators or only allocate funds among general asset types without recommending specific products. Existing AFS licensees may not need to change licensing arrangements but must review compliance. Start-up fintech businesses must obtain their own licence or become authorised representatives.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital advice providers must determine if they qualify for exemptions from AFS licensing, which affects data transfer and processing obligations. This section clarifies when licensing is required for digital advice services.

requirements.text: Digital advice providers may be exempt from AFS licensing if they provide generic financial calculators
Digital advice providers may be exempt from AFS licensing if they only allocate funds among general asset types without recommending specific products
Existing AFS licensees may not need to change licensing arrangements to provide digital advice
Start-up fintech businesses must obtain their own AFS licence or become authorised representatives

sections.source_quote: Depending on your circumstances, you may be exempt or have relief from the requirement to hold an AFS licence: see 911A(2) and RG 36.34.
sections.relevance_reason: AFS licensing affects data handling but not direct data protection

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Becoming an authorised representative for digital advice services
sections.summary: Digital advice providers considering becoming authorised representatives must obtain written authorisation from an AFS licensee. They must consider the type of financial services, required licence authorisations, and compliance assistance needs. AFS licensees must have appropriate licence authorisations to provide financial product advice to clients.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital advice providers must obtain written authorisation from an AFS licensee to act as an authorised representative, which affects data transfer and processing obligations. This section outlines the requirements for becoming an authorised representative.

requirements.text: Digital advice providers must obtain written authorisation from an AFS licensee to act as an authorised representative
Digital advice providers must consider the type of financial services they intend to offer
Digital advice providers must consider the required licence authorisations for their business
Digital advice providers must consider compliance assistance needs

sections.source_quote: If you would like to provide a financial service, such as digital advice, as an authorised representative on behalf of an AFS licensee, you must hold a written authorisation from that licensee: see s916A of the Corporations Act.
sections.relevance_reason: regulator powers for financial services

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital advice provider assessment questions for AFS licensees
sections.summary: This section outlines sample questions for prospective Australian Financial Services (AFS) licensees to assess their capability in providing digital financial advice. It covers business model, revenue sources, license scope, experience with innovation, algorithm testing, representative numbers, compliance assistance, training provision, monitoring capabilities, record-keeping procedures, past regulatory notifications, and enforcement history. The questions aim to evaluate whether the licensee can meet regulatory obligations for digital advice services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section identifies key questions to assess AFS licensees' capacity for digital advice, including algorithm testing and compliance monitoring, which are critical for data transfer impact assessments involving algorithmic financial advice systems.

requirements.text: What is your main financial services business?
Do you operate your own financial services business or only obtain revenue by appointing authorised representatives?
Does your AFS licence include authorisations needed to provide digital advice and related services?
What experience do you have with innovation and digital advice?
What monitoring and testing will you carry out in relation to advice algorithms?
How many other authorised representatives do you have?
What compliance assistance will you provide?
Will you provide my business with any training?
Will you provide enough monitoring and supervision to ensure my business operates within the law?
Do you have compliance procedures for record keeping?
How many notifications of reportable situations have you lodged with ASIC in the past two years?
Have you been the subject of any ASIC or other regulatory agency's surveillance, investigation, enforceable undertaking or enforcement action?

sections.source_quote: Sample questions to ask prospective AFS licensees • What is your main financial services business? • Do you operate your own financial services business or do you only obtain your revenue by appointing authorised representatives? • Does your AFS licence include the authorisations that my business needs to provide digital advice and any related services? • What experience do you have with innovation and digital advice? • What monitoring and testing will you carry out in relation to my advice algorithms? • How many other authorised representatives do you have? • What compliance assistance will you provide? • Will you provide my business with any training? • Will you provide enough monitoring and supervision to ensure that my business is operating within the law? • Do you have compliance procedures for record keeping? • How many notifications of reportable situations have you lodged with ASIC in the past two years? • Have you been the subject of any ASIC or other regulatory agency's surveillance, investigation, enforceable undertaking or enforcement action?
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory assessment for digital advice compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Additional information required for AFS licensees providing digital advice
sections.summary: This section specifies additional information AFS licensees must provide to ASIC when applying for a license to offer digital financial advice. It covers human resources with technological knowledge, human review for personal advice, outsourcing measures, algorithm monitoring procedures, record-keeping arrangements, risk management and security systems, and professional indemnity insurance adequacy. The requirements ensure digital advice providers meet regulatory standards for client data handling and service delivery.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details specific information needed for digital advice providers to ensure compliance with data handling and security requirements, which are critical for data transfer impact assessments involving algorithmic financial advice.

requirements.text: Provide information about human resources with technological knowledge and skills to understand algorithms and review digital advice
Specify the level of human review for personal advice generated by algorithms
Describe measures to ensure due skill and care in selecting service providers when outsourcing functions
Outline procedures to monitor ongoing performance of service providers
Detail procedures for monitoring and testing algorithms
Explain arrangements for complying with record-keeping obligations
Describe risk management and security arrangements for client information storage and transmission
Explain how professional indemnity insurance cover is determined to be adequate

sections.source_quote: RG 255.45: RG 2 explains what information you need to provide to ASIC as part of your AFS licence application. Depending on the nature, scale and complexity of your business, we may ask you for additional information about: (a) the human resources you will have with the technological knowledge and skills to: (i) generally understand the technology and algorithms used to provide the digital advice; and (ii) review the digital advice generated by algorithms (see RG 255.60–RG 255.64); (b) if you are providing personal advice, the level of human review that will be undertaken on the advice generated (see RG 255.109–RG 255.112); (c) if you outsource any functions, the measures you will put in place to ensure that: (i) due skill and care are taken in choosing suitable service providers; and (ii) you can and will monitor the ongoing performance of service providers (see RG 255.67–RG 255.68); (d) the procedures you have in place to monitor and test algorithms (see RG 255.72–RG 255.74); (e) the arrangements you will put in place to comply with your record-keeping obligations; (f) the risk management and security arrangements you will have in place to ensure that client information is stored and transmitted securely (see RG 255.70–RG 255.79); and (g) how you have determined that your professional indemnity (PI) insurance cover is adequate (see RG 255.80–RG 255.85).
sections.relevance_reason: Core data security and cross-border compliance for financial services

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Organisational competence requirements for digital advice licensees
sections.summary: This section details the organisational competence obligations for AFS licensees providing digital financial advice. It specifies that digital advice licensees must have at least one responsible manager who meets training and competence standards, as there is no natural person directly involved in providing advice. The requirements ensure that digital advice providers maintain appropriate knowledge and skills to operate within the law, with specific focus on algorithmic advice systems.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses the organisational competence requirements for digital advice providers, which are critical for ensuring that algorithmic advice systems meet regulatory standards and do not pose risks to client data.

requirements.text: Maintain competence to provide financial services covered by their licence
Ensure representatives are adequately trained and competent to provide financial services
Demonstrate that each responsible manager meets one of five options for appropriate knowledge and skills
Ensure responsible managers collectively have appropriate knowledge and skills to cover all financial services and products
In digital advice contexts, there is no natural person directly involved in providing advice, so training and competence standards do not apply
Digital advice licensees must have at least one responsible manager who meets training and competence standards

sections.source_quote: RG 255.48: Under the Corporations Act, AFS licensees are required to: (a) maintain competence to provide the financial services covered by their licence (s912A(1)(e)); and (b) ensure that their representatives are adequately trained and competent to provide those financial services (s912A(1)(f)).
sections.relevance_reason: Organisational competence for digital advice licensees

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Responsible manager competence for digital advice licensees
sections.summary: This section explains that in most cases, existing AFS licensees already have a responsible manager who meets training and competence standards. However, in some instances, a licensee may not have a responsible manager who meets these standards, particularly if the responsible manager previously demonstrated competence through relevant experience over the past 10 years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies the competence requirements for responsible managers in digital advice licensees, which is relevant for ensuring that algorithmic advice systems are operated within regulatory frameworks.

requirements.text: At least one responsible manager of an existing AFS licensee will meet the training and competence standards
In some instances, a licensee may not have a responsible manager who meets training and competence standards
Responsible managers who previously demonstrated competence through relevant experience over the past 10 years may not meet current standards

sections.source_quote: RG 255.54: In most cases, at least one responsible manager of an existing AFS licensee will meet the training and competence standards. In a small number of instances, however, a licensee may not have a responsible manager who meets these standards. This is because the responsible manager was previously able to demonstrate their competence under RG 105 by showing they had relevant experience over the previous 10 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Supporting: responsible manager competence for financial data

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: General obligations for digital advice licensees
sections.summary: This section outlines the general obligations that apply to all AFS licensees, including the requirement to have adequate resources, establish risk management systems, and comply with financial services laws. It specifies that digital advice licensees must meet these obligations, with additional guidance on human resources, technological capabilities, and risk management to ensure compliance with the Corporations Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details the general obligations for digital advice licensees, which are critical for ensuring that algorithmic advice systems comply with regulatory requirements and data handling standards.

requirements.text: Have adequate financial, technological and human resources to provide financial services and carry out supervisory arrangements
Establish and maintain adequate risk management systems
Comply with financial services laws
Take reasonable steps to ensure representatives comply with financial services laws
Maintain competence to provide financial services
Ensure representatives are adequately trained and competent to provide financial services

sections.source_quote: RG 255.55: If you are an AFS licensee, you have general obligations under s912A(1) of the Corporations Act to: (a) do all things necessary to ensure that the financial services covered by your licence are provided efficiently, honestly and fairly (s912A(1)(a)); (b) have adequate arrangements in place for managing conflicts of interest (s912A(1)(aa)); (c) comply with the conditions on your licence (s912A(1)(b)); (d) comply with the financial services laws (s912A(1)(c)); (e) take reasonable steps to ensure that your representatives comply with the financial services laws (s912A(1)(ca)); (f) have adequate financial, technological and human resources to provide the financial services covered by your licence and to carry out supervisory arrangements (s912A(1)(d)); (g) maintain the competence to provide the financial services covered by your licence (s912A(1)(e)); (h) ensure that your representatives are adequately trained and competent to provide those financial services (s912A(1)(f)); (i) if you provide financial services to retail clients, have a dispute resolution system (s912A(1)(g)); and (j) establish and maintain adequate risk management systems (s912A(1)(h)).
sections.relevance_reason: Supporting: risk management systems, resources for compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Resource requirements for digital advice licensees
sections.summary: This section focuses on the resource requirements for AFS licensees providing digital financial advice, emphasizing the need for adequate human resources to understand algorithms and review digital advice outputs. It specifies that digital advice licensees must have individuals within their business who can comprehend the technology and algorithms used to provide advice and assess the generated advice for compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses the human resource requirements for digital advice licensees, which are critical for ensuring that algorithmic advice systems are properly monitored and compliant with regulatory standards.

requirements.text: Ensure there are people within the business who understand the technology and algorithms used to provide digital advice
Ensure there are people within the business who can review digital advice generated by algorithms

sections.source_quote: RG 255.60: As a digital advice licensee, you should ensure that there are people within your business who: (a) have an understanding of the technology and algorithms used to provide digital advice; and (b) are able to review the digital advice generated by algorithms.
sections.relevance_reason: Resource requirements for security compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital advice licensees must have staff understanding algorithms and technology
sections.summary: Digital advice licensees must have at least one person who understands the technology and algorithms used for digital advice. This person does not need to understand the specific computer coding but must comprehend the rationale, risks, and rules behind the algorithms. The requirement applies to all digital advice licensees regardless of business size, with the extent of human resources depending on the scale of operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital advice licensees must ensure staff can review algorithmic advice for compliance, which impacts data transfer assessments by requiring technical oversight of algorithmic systems.

requirements.text: Digital advice licensees must have at least one person who understands the technology and algorithms used for digital advice
This person must comprehend the rationale, risks, and rules behind the algorithms without needing to understand specific computer coding

sections.source_quote: RG 255.61: Digital advice is provided through technology... we expect digital advice licensees to have at least one person who has a general understanding of the technology and algorithms...
sections.relevance_reason: Staff understanding algorithms for financial data compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital advice licensees must monitor outsourced algorithm functions
sections.summary: Digital advice licensees outsourcing algorithm functions must ensure suitable service providers are chosen and monitored. Licensees remain responsible for financial services provided to clients even when functions are outsourced. This includes understanding algorithm rationale, risks, and rules without needing to code the algorithms.
sections.dtia_summary: Outsourcing algorithm functions requires licensees to maintain oversight of third-party providers to ensure compliance, affecting data transfer assessments through third-party data handling risks.

requirements.text: Digital advice licensees must choose service providers with due skill and care for outsourced functions
Digital advice licensees must monitor ongoing performance of outsourced service providers
Digital advice licensees remain responsible for financial services provided to clients even when functions are outsourced

sections.source_quote: RG 255.67: We expect that you will have measures in place to ensure that due skill and care are taken in choosing suitable outsourced service providers; and you can and will monitor the ongoing performance of outsourced service providers.
sections.relevance_reason: Third-party monitoring for financial data compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital advice licensees must monitor and test algorithms regularly
sections.summary: Digital advice licensees must regularly monitor and test algorithms to ensure compliance. This includes having system design documentation, documented test strategies, processes for managing algorithm changes, records of algorithm changes over seven years, and mechanisms to suspend advice if errors are detected. These requirements apply to all digital advice licensees regardless of business scale.
sections.dtia_summary: Algorithm monitoring and testing requirements directly impact data transfer assessments by mandating technical oversight of algorithmic systems to ensure compliance with financial regulations.

requirements.text: Digital advice licensees must have system design documentation that clearly sets out the purpose, scope, and design of algorithms
Digital advice licensees must have a documented test strategy explaining the scope of algorithm testing
Digital advice licensees must have processes for managing changes to algorithms with security arrangements to prevent unauthorized access
Digital advice licensees must control, monitor, and retain records of algorithm changes for seven years
Digital advice licensees must review and update algorithms when factors affecting their currency change
Digital advice licensees must suspend advice provision if algorithm errors are detected that could cause client loss or breach of the Corporations Act
Digital advice licensees must have adequate resources to monitor and supervise algorithm performance
Digital advice licensees must have an internal sign-off process to ensure compliance with algorithm monitoring requirements

sections.source_quote: RG 255.72: As part of your risk management systems, you should regularly monitor and test the algorithms that underpin the advice.
sections.relevance_reason: Algorithm monitoring for financial data compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital advice licensees must implement cyber security frameworks
sections.summary: Digital advice licensees must assess cyber security using recognized frameworks like NIST or Australian Signals Directorate strategies. They must also comply with information security standards such as ISO/IEC 27001 and have adequate cloud security measures to mitigate malicious cyber activity risks.
sections.dtia_summary: Cyber security requirements directly impact data transfer assessments by mandating robust security frameworks for digital advice systems, affecting cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Digital advice licensees must assess cyber security using recognized frameworks such as NIST or Australian Signals Directorate strategies
Digital advice licensees must comply with information security standards including ISO/IEC 27001:2015
Digital advice licensees must implement adequate cloud security measures to mitigate malicious cyber activity

sections.source_quote: RG 255.77: We expect digital advice licensees to assess their cyber security using recognised frameworks, such as the National Institute of Standards and Technology's Framework for improving critical infrastructure cybersecurity...
sections.relevance_reason: Cyber security frameworks for financial data

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital advice licensees must have adequate compensation arrangements
sections.summary: Digital advice licensees must have arrangements for compensating clients for losses resulting from breaches of obligations under the Corporations Act. These arrangements must either satisfy professional indemnity insurance requirements or be approved by ASIC as alternative arrangements, considering factors like client growth, potential widespread losses, and claim aggregation.
sections.dtia_summary: Compensation arrangements requirements impact data transfer assessments by mandating financial safeguards for client losses, affecting cross-border data handling risks.

requirements.text: Digital advice licensees must have arrangements for compensating clients for losses from breaches of obligations under the Corporations Act
Compensation arrangements must satisfy professional indemnity insurance requirements or be approved by ASIC as alternatives
Digital advice licensees must regularly review business risks to assess PI insurance adequacy
Digital advice licensees must notify PI insurers of material business changes
Digital advice licensees must review PI insurance policy features at least annually

sections.source_quote: RG 255.80: Under s912B, AFS licensees that provide financial services to retail clients must have arrangements for compensating clients for loss they suffer as a result of a breach by the licensee or its representatives of their obligations in Ch 7 of the Corporations Act.
sections.relevance_reason: PI insurance for financial data compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital advice must comply with best interests duty
sections.summary: Digital advice providers must act in the best interests of clients when providing advice, as the law is technology neutral. This obligation applies to all digital advice providers, including those using computer programs, and requires adherence to the same standards as traditional financial advice.
sections.dtia_summary: Best interests duty requirements impact data transfer assessments by mandating client-centric advice practices, affecting data handling and processing across borders.

requirements.text: Digital advice providers must act in the best interests of clients when providing advice

sections.source_quote: RG 255.87: When providing personal advice to a retail client, advice providers must act in the best interests of the client in relation to that advice: s961B(1).
sections.relevance_reason: Best interests duty for financial advice

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital advice provider obligations for retail clients
sections.summary: Digital advice providers must provide appropriate advice, warn clients about incomplete or inaccurate information, and prioritize client interests over their own or associates' interests when giving personal financial advice. These obligations apply to all personal advice but exclude general advice. The guidance specifies that scaled advice (limited scope personal advice) must be handled with particular care due to the absence of direct client interaction in digital contexts.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital advice providers must clearly communicate the scope of advice, obtain client consent for limited advice, and ensure advice remains within client's best interests to avoid cross-border data transfer risks.

requirements.text: Provide appropriate financial advice to retail clients
Warn clients if advice is based on incomplete or inaccurate information
Prioritize client's interests over own interests or associates' interests

sections.source_quote: RG 255.90: A digital advice provider giving personal advice to a client also has related obligations in Div 2 of Pt 7.7A of the Corporations Act to provide appropriate advice, to warn the client if advice is based on incomplete or inaccurate information, and to prioritise the client's interests over its own interests or that of the advice provider's associates.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial advice obligations with data implications

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Minimum expectations for digital scaled advice
sections.summary: Digital advice providers must explain the scope of advice upfront, require clients to actively demonstrate advice relevance, inform about limitations and consequences at key points, provide key concepts and risks/benefits, filter inappropriate clients, disclose costs, withdrawal options, dispute resolution, and explain benefits of following advice. These minimum expectations ensure scaled advice remains in the client's best interests without direct interaction.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital advice providers must implement clear communication protocols to define advice scope and ensure client understanding, critical for data transfer compliance in cross-border financial contexts.

requirements.text: Explain advice scope and what is not offered from the outset
Require client to actively demonstrate advice relevance
Inform about limitations and consequences at key points
Inform about key concepts and associated risks/benefits
Filter clients for whom advice is inappropriate
Disclose upfront and ongoing costs of advice
Inform about withdrawal options and associated costs
Explain dispute resolution processes
Explain why client will be in better position following advice

sections.source_quote: RG 255.98: As a minimum, you should: explain to the client from the outset what advice is being offered and what is not being offered (i.e. the scope of the advice); require the client to actively demonstrate that the advice they are seeking is within the scope of what is being offered by the digital advice model; at key points in the advice process, inform the client about the limitations and potential consequences of the scope of advice; throughout the advice process, inform the client about key concepts and the relevant risks and benefits associated with the advice being provided; filter out clients for whom the advice being offered is not appropriate, or who want advice on a topic outside the scope of advice being offered; inform the client about the upfront and ongoing costs of the advice before the advice is given or implemented; inform the client about how they can withdraw from the advice being provided, and any associated costs, before the advice is implemented; explain what dispute resolution processes are available to the client if they wish to make a complaint; and explain why the client is likely to be in a better position if they follow the advice.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data handling and client disclosures

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Example of scaled digital advice in client's best interests
sections.summary: The example demonstrates that digital advice is in the client's best interests when it clearly identifies advice scope, explains consequences of limited advice, obtains client consent, provides ongoing reinforcement of scope limitations, and filters clients based on relevant circumstances. This ensures advice remains appropriate and within legal scope without direct interaction.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital advice must clearly define scope, obtain explicit consent, and implement ongoing client communication to ensure advice remains within client's best interests during data transfers.

requirements.text: Clearly identify what advice is provided and what is not
Explain consequences of proceeding with scaled advice
Obtain client consent to proceed with scaled advice
Provide pop-up information to reinforce limited scope and implications
Identify whether client's circumstances are consistent with advice scope

sections.source_quote: RG 255.99: In Example 5, we consider that the digital advice provider provides scaled advice that is in the best interests of the client.
sections.relevance_reason: Supporting: consent, scope limitations, client reinforcement

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Triage and filtering process for digital advice
sections.summary: Digital advice providers must implement a robust triage or filtering process to identify clients for whom advice is inappropriate. This includes testing at key points in the advice process whether advice is appropriate and in the client's best interests, filtering clients who seek advice outside scope, and handling inconsistent client responses through clarification or exclusion.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital advice providers must actively filter clients to ensure advice remains within scope and in client's best interests, critical for data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: Ensure robust triage or filtering process to identify inappropriate clients
Test at key points whether advice is appropriate and in client's best interests
Filter clients seeking advice outside scope of digital advice
Identify inconsistencies in client responses and provide clarification or exclusion

sections.source_quote: RG 255.102: If a client seeks advice on an area outside the scope of the advice being offered, the client should be filtered out of the digital advice model.
sections.relevance_reason: Supporting process for financial advice compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Combined financial advice model example
sections.summary: A combined model uses digital advice tools for initial assessment and human advisers for complex cases. The digital tool filters clients based on circumstances, and human advisers verify information and provide tailored advice, ensuring compliance with best interests obligations and SOA requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital advice models must include mechanisms to identify and handle complex client cases to maintain data transfer compliance and client protection.

requirements.text: Use digital advice tool for initial assessment and human adviser for complex cases
Verify client information with client
Identify information gaps or inconsistencies
Provide advice considering all client circumstances

sections.source_quote: RG 255.106: In Example 6, a digital advice licensee provides a combined (or hybrid) financial product advice model. The licensee correctly filters the client out of the digital advice tool because the advice would not be appropriate for the client's more complex personal circumstances.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data handling with compliance checks

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital strategic advice without product recommendations
sections.summary: Digital advice providers must ensure clients understand advice scope, obtain consent, and confirm data completeness before generating SOAs. This includes verifying client circumstances and readiness to proceed with advice, critical for maintaining compliance in digital contexts.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital advice providers must confirm client data completeness and readiness before finalizing advice to ensure data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: Confirm client has finished inputting data and is ready to proceed
Provide SOA after confirming client circumstances are up to date and accurate

sections.source_quote: RG 255.107: In a digital advice context, it may not always be clear when a client has finished inputting their data into the digital advice tool. This is because a client may 'play around' and change the variables of the tool.
sections.relevance_reason: Core financial data compliance with consent and SOA requirements

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital advice algorithm monitoring and error correction
sections.summary: Digital advice licensees must conduct frequent reviews of algorithms initially and after any changes, including testing scenarios to ensure advice quality. They must monitor algorithms through periodic and random reviews. If errors are detected that could cause client loss or breach the Corporations Act, licensees must immediately rectify the issues and stop providing advice until fixed. They must also have procedures to identify and contact clients affected by inappropriate advice.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires robust algorithm testing and error correction protocols for digital advice, critical for ensuring data transfers via algorithms comply with regulatory safeguards and client protection standards.

requirements.text: Conduct frequent reviews of digital advice algorithms initially and after any changes
Test scenarios to ensure quality of advice after algorithm changes
Monitor algorithms through periodic and random advice reviews
Immediately rectify algorithm errors that could cause client loss or breach the Corporations Act
Stop providing advice until algorithm defects are rectified
Have procedures to identify and contact clients affected by inappropriate advice

sections.source_quote: Frequent reviews of digital advice should be conducted initially, and with heightened scrutiny when any change to an algorithm is made. When changes are made, it would be prudent to run a number of test scenarios to test the quality of advice provided. Algorithms should be regularly monitored and tested through periodic and random advice reviews.
sections.relevance_reason: Algorithm monitoring for financial data compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Relevant ASIC regulatory guides for digital advice
sections.summary: This section lists regulatory guides that may be relevant to digital advice, including AFS licensing, product disclosure, training, and conflict of interest guidance. It emphasizes reading other ASIC guides for comprehensive compliance with financial services regulations.

requirements.text: Read other relevant ASIC regulatory guides for comprehensive compliance

sections.source_quote: This regulatory guide should be read in conjunction with other relevant regulatory guides issued by ASIC.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: general compliance guidance for financial services

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Additional ASIC guidance on digital advice compliance
sections.summary: This section details specific ASIC guidance on discretionary powers, product disclosure, financial product advisers, managed discretionary accounts, and conflicts of interest relevant to digital advice. It outlines obligations for managing conflicts and ensuring proper disclosure practices.

requirements.text: Follow guidance on discretionary powers for compliance with Corporations Act
Comply with product disclosure obligations for digital advice
Adhere to conduct and disclosure obligations for financial product advisers
Regulate managed discretionary account services for retail clients
Manage conflicts of interest as required by s912A(1)(aa) of the Corporations Act

sections.source_quote: RG 175 Licensing: Financial product advisers—Conduct and disclosure: This is a guide for persons who provide financial product advice to retail clients, and their professional advisers (e.g. lawyers).
sections.relevance_reason: financial product compliance guidance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital advice fee and remuneration compliance
sections.summary: This section covers guidance on scaled advice, conflicted remuneration, and ongoing fee arrangements for digital advice. It specifies obligations for fee disclosures, annual renewals, and written consent for fee deductions.

requirements.text: Provide annual fee disclosure statements to clients
Seek annual renewal of ongoing fee arrangements
Obtain client written consent to deduct ongoing fees

sections.source_quote: INFO 256 FAQs: Ongoing fee arrangements: This information sheet contains frequently asked questions (FAQs) that explain the obligations in Div 3 of Pt 7.7A and Pt 10.46 of the Corporations Act which apply to persons who provide personal advice to retail clients under an ongoing fee arrangement.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data compliance with consent and disclosures

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Key terms for digital advice compliance
sections.summary: This section defines critical terms such as digital advice, digital advice licensee, and financial product advice, clarifying their meanings within the context of the Corporations Act and regulatory obligations. These definitions are essential for understanding compliance requirements.

requirements.text: Digital advice is defined as automated financial product advice using algorithms without direct human involvement
Digital advice licensee is an AFS licensee offering digital advice to retail clients
Financial product advice is a recommendation or statement of opinion intended to influence financial product decisions

sections.source_quote: digital advice: Also known as 'robo-advice' or 'automated advice'—the provision of automated financial product advice using algorithms and technology and without the direct involvement of a human adviser
sections.relevance_reason: defines key terms for compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Additional key terms for digital advice
sections.summary: This section provides further definitions of terms like client's relevant circumstances, Corporations Act, and financial product advice, which are crucial for understanding the scope and application of digital advice regulations.

requirements.text: Client's relevant circumstances include objectives, financial situation, and needs
Corporations Act refers to the Corporations Act 2001 and related regulations
Financial product advice is a recommendation or statement of opinion intended to influence financial product decisions

sections.source_quote: client's relevant circumstances: The objectives, financial situation and needs of a client that would reasonably be considered relevant to the subject matter of advice sought by the client
sections.relevance_reason: tangential definitions for financial advice

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Definitions for digital financial advice and AFS licensees
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in providing digital financial product advice to retail clients. It specifies that general advice is non-personal advice, personal advice requires consideration of client objectives and financial situation, and outlines the roles of responsible managers, AFS licensees, and other critical entities. These definitions support compliance with the Corporations Act and ASIC regulatory requirements.

requirements.text: General advice is financial product advice that is not personal advice
Personal advice is financial product advice given or directed to a person in circumstances where the provider has considered one or more of the client's objectives, financial situation and needs
Responsible managers are persons nominated by an AFS licensee who are directly responsible for significant day-to-day decisions about the ongoing provision of financial services
Responsible managers must have appropriate knowledge and skills to ensure the licensee can provide financial services for which it has been authorised

sections.source_quote: Term: general advice - Financial product advice that is not personal advice. Term: personal advice - Financial product advice given or directed to a person (including by electronic means) in circumstances where: the provider of the advice has considered one or more of the client's objectives, financial situation and needs; or a reasonable person might expect the provider to have considered one or more of these matters
sections.relevance_reason: Defines critical terms for financial advice compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Digital advice documentation and best interests standards
sections.summary: This section defines terms related to digital financial advice, including scaled advice as personal advice limited in scope, and the safe harbour for the best interests duty which refers to specific steps under the Corporations Act. It also specifies that a Statement of Advice (SOA) must be provided to clients for personal advice under certain conditions.

requirements.text: Scaled advice is personal advice that is limited in scope
Safe harbour for the best interests duty refers to the steps set out in s961B(2) of the Corporations Act
A Statement of Advice (SOA) must be given to a client for the provision of personal advice under Subdivs C and D of Div 3 of Pt 7.7 of the Corporations Act

sections.source_quote: Term: scaled advice - Personal advice that is limited in scope. Term: safe harbour for the best interests duty - The steps set out in s961B(2) of the Corporations Act. Term: SOA (Statement of Advice) - A document that must be given to a client for the provision of personal advice under Subdivs C and D of Div 3 of Pt 7.7 of the Corporations Act
sections.relevance_reason: Definitions of financial advice terms

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Related information and legislative references for digital advice
sections.summary: This section lists related information and legislative references for digital financial advice, including headnotes on key terms, relevant legislation such as the Corporations Act, and regulatory instruments like ASIC's Corporations (Generic Calculators) Instrument and Product Disclosure Statements. It identifies specific regulations and guidance documents that support compliance with digital advice requirements.

requirements.text: Headnotes include advisers, AFS licensees, algorithms, automated advice, best interests duty, and other relevant terms
Legislation includes the Corporations Act, Ch 7, Pt 7.7, and specific sections such as 761G, 766B, 912A(1), and others
ASIC publications include the Corporations (Generic Calculators) Instrument 2016/207 and Corporations (Facilitating Electronic Delivery of Financial Services Disclosure) Instrument 2015/647

sections.source_quote: Headnotes and legislation: advisers, AFS licensees, algorithms, authorised representatives, automated advice, best interests duty, compensation arrangements, compliance, cyber security, digital advice, exchange-traded funds, factual information, financial product advice, fintech, general advice, licensing, managed discretionary account, monitoring and testing, organisational competence, outsourcing, personal advice, record-keeping obligations, responsible manager, retail client, robo-advice, scaled advice, start-up business, technology, training and competence standards, triage or filtering process
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory instruments and definitions for financial data compliance

sections.heading: REGULATORY GUIDE 255: Providing digital financial product advice to retail clients
sections.title: Regulatory guides and reports for digital advice compliance
sections.summary: This section lists regulatory guides and reports relevant to digital financial advice, including ASIC's Regulatory Guide 3, RG 36, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 126, RG 146, RG 167, RG 168, RG 175, RG 179, RG 221, RG 244, and specific reports on cyber resilience. These resources provide detailed guidance on compliance with digital advice requirements and cybersecurity standards.

requirements.text: Regulatory guides include RG 3, RG 36, RG 78, RG 104, RG 105, RG 126, RG 146, RG 167, RG 168, RG 175, RG 179, RG 221, RG 244
Reports include REP 429 Cyber resilience: Health check and REP 468 Cyber resilience assessment report: ASX Group and Chi-X Australia Pty Ltd

sections.source_quote: Regulatory guides: RG 3 AFS Licensing Kit: Part 3—Preparing your additional proofs, RG 36 Licensing: Financial product advice and dealing, RG 78 Breach reporting by AFS licensees and credit licensees, RG 104 AFS licensing: Meeting the general obligations, RG 105 AFS licensing: Organisational competence, RG 126 Compensation and insurance arrangements for AFS licensees, RG 146 Licensing: Training of financial product advisers, RG 167 Licensing: Discretionary powers, RG 168 Disclosure: Product Disclosure Statements (and other disclosure obligations), RG 175 Licensing: Financial product advisers—Conduct and disclosure, RG 179 Managed discretionary account services, RG 221 Facilitating digital financial services disclosures, RG 244 Giving information, general advice and scaled advice
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory guides for cyber resilience
